Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T76 (ledgerRefs goals:G5)
Branch: implement/T76    Base commit: 1a757b1 (current main HEAD). If cut from a stale HEAD, `git reset --hard 1a757b1` first; confirm internal/device has the up()/startResolution resolver-factory seam (T74) + internal/dnsresolve.FakeResolver, and test/e2e/p5_dpi_test.go exists before starting.
headline: Add DPI-posture guard tests: opt-in OFF means zero DNS and an unchanged wire audit
description: Operationalize the Q29/Q33 security acceptance target. (1) Unit-level guard: with any all-IP-literal config, assert no dnsresolve.Resolver is constructed and no resolution goroutine starts (a TRIPWIRE fake resolver that FAILS the test if Lookup is ever called, injected at the wiring seam — the up()/startResolution resolver-factory seam). (2) Wire-audit guard: the existing p5 DPI test (test/e2e p5_dpi_test.go) must pass unchanged on a DNS-disabled config — assert the tunnel wire is byte-identical in shape to pre-G5 (the audit already encodes this; extend it if needed to assert zero port-53/DoH/DoT egress from the edge netns while DNS is off). (3) Documentation hook: the guard test names the exact leaked artifact per mode (system: cleartext DNS query naming the concentrator; DoH/DoT: TLS SNI + timing to the resolver) so the docs task can cite a tested statement.
acceptance: go test ./internal/device/... passes the tripwire-resolver case; the p5 DPI e2e (go test -tags e2e -run P5 on the e2e hosts) passes unchanged on a DNS-off config, extended with a zero-DNS-egress assertion for the edge namespace.

IMPORTANT: the -tags e2e P5 suite needs privileged hardware you CANNOT run here — the tripwire-resolver UNIT test (internal/device) IS runnable and is the core deliverable; make it pass. For the e2e extension, ensure it compiles/vets under -tags e2e (`go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e` + `go build -tags e2e ./test/e2e`); note e2e execution deferred (G2 pattern). The KEY property: the tripwire fake resolver's Lookup must FAIL the test if EVER called on an all-IP-literal config (proving Q29 zero-DNS inertness). Mutation-verify: wiring a resolver unconditionally makes the tripwire test fail.

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes. Read internal/device up()/startResolution (T74's resolver-factory seam + the zero-hostname inertness path — T74 already proved factoryCalls==0 for literal configs; T76 hardens that with a tripwire that FAILS on Lookup) and test/e2e/p5_dpi_test.go.
If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t76-fix` off 1a757b1 in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

220		// guard in up(): with tun_persist=true the kernel keeps wanbond0 (and its
221		// operator-owned addresses/routes) across a daemon restart; with the default
222		// false it explicitly CLEARS the flag so the device disappears on Close exactly
223		// as before. This composes with the ifUp above and does not touch the reload or
224		// restart-on-failure paths — a reload never re-creates the TUN, and a persistent
225		// device is simply re-adopted by name on the next Up (see setTUNPersist).
226		if err := setTUNPersist(tunDev, cfg.TUNPersist); err != nil {
227			_ = tunDev.Close()
228			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: set TUN persistence: %w", err)
229		}
230		clg.Info("interface up", "interface", name, "tun_persist", cfg.TUNPersist)
231		// The DNS re-resolution transport is the one the (validated) [dns] block selects. It is passed
232		// as a factory (not eagerly constructed) so up() builds it AT MOST ONCE and ONLY when some peer
233		// carries a hostname endpoint spec — a config with zero hostname specs never constructs a
234		// resolver (Q29 inertness). The injected TUN and factory are also the seams device tests drive.
235		return up(cfg, clg, tunDev, name, cfg.DNS.NewResolver)
236	}
237	
238	// resolverFactory builds the DNS resolver used for hostname endpoint re-resolution. It is injected
239	// so a device test can supply a fake; production passes cfg.DNS.NewResolver. up() invokes it AT
240	// MOST ONCE, and ONLY when some peer carries a hostname endpoint spec.
241	type resolverFactory func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error)
242	
243	// up drives the tunnel bring-up over an already-created TUN and an injected resolver factory: it
244	// wires the multipath Bind into the amneziawg engine, performs the bounded initial hostname
245	// resolve (Q30), applies the crypto/endpoint UAPI config built ONLY from resolved entries, brings
246	// the device up, and starts the probe / reconcile / hub-failover / re-resolution loops. Splitting
247	// it out of Up gives device tests a seam to inject a channel TUN and a fake resolver without the
248	// privileged tun.CreateTUN. The same path drives both roles; the role only changes which UAPI
249	// fields cfg carries (the concentrator sets listen_port; the edge sets each peer's endpoint).
250	func up(cfg *config.Config, clg log.Logger, tunDev tun.Device, name string, newResolver resolverFactory) (*Tunnel, error) {
251		// Claim the single-amnezia-engine-per-process invariant (D2) BEFORE IpcSet
252		// assigns amneziawg-go's process-global message-type state. On any failure
253		// below, ok stays false and the deferred release returns the hold; the
254		// successful path transfers the hold to the returned Tunnel.
255		if err := globalAmneziaGuard.acquire(cfg.Amnezia); err != nil {
256			_ = tunDev.Close()
257			return nil, err
258		}
259		ok := false
260		defer func() {
261			if !ok {
262				globalAmneziaGuard.release(cfg.Amnezia)
263			}
264		}()
265	
266		// One random per-boot probe session id, shared by every path AND every configured peer (it
267		// identifies THIS boot, not a path or peer): a peer restart presents a new id that resets
268		// the surviving responder's anti-replay high-water so liveness recovers (T38, D12), and a
269		// runtime-added path (T30) reuses it so its probes join this boot's stream.
270		sessionID, err := telemetry.NewSessionID(rand.Reader)
271		if err != nil {
272			_ = tunDev.Close()
273			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: new probe session id: %w", err)
274		}
275		// PeerIdentities is the SINGLE place the single-peer/multi-peer effective-PSK decision is
276		// made (G4): for a single-peer config every identity's PSK is the top-level psk; for a
277		// multi-peer concentrator each is that peer's own psk. Its order matches cfg.WireGuard.Peers
278		// (and thus uapiConfig's peer render), so the engine peer set and the Bind peer set agree.
279		ids := cfg.PeerIdentities()
280		// The primary peer (peers[0]) drives on-wire liveness through the SAME prober values that
281		// back its scheduler's PathHealth (T37). It is keyed on the FIRST identity's effective psk:
282		// the top-level psk for a single-peer config (byte-identical to pre-G4), or peer 0's own psk
283		// for a multi-peer concentrator.
284		scheduler, probers, newProber, err := buildScheduler(cfg, ids[0].PSK, sessionID, clg)
285		if err != nil {
286			_ = tunDev.Close()
287			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build scheduler: %w", err)
288		}
289		mpBind, err := bind.NewMultipath(cfg.Paths, ids[0].PSK, scheduler, probers, newProber, fecConfig(cfg.FEC), adaptiveFECConfig(cfg.FEC), cfg.Amnezia)
290		if err != nil {
291			_ = tunDev.Close()
292			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build multipath bind: %w", err)
293		}
294		// Concentrator per-peer wiring (G4/T93): register each ADDITIONAL configured peer with its
295		// OWN prober set, scheduler, and prober factory (all keyed on that peer's effective psk),
296		// BEFORE dev.Up drives the bind's Open — which builds each registered peer's per-(peer,path)
297		// view of every bound socket, reconciles its scheduler, and reports its stable virtual
298		// endpoint to the engine (A1). A single-peer config has exactly one identity, so this loop
299		// is empty and the wiring stays byte-identical to the pre-G4 single-peer path.
300		for _, id := range ids[1:] {
301			psched, pprobers, pfactory, perr := buildScheduler(cfg, id.PSK, sessionID, clg)
302			if perr != nil {
303				_ = tunDev.Close()
304				return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build scheduler for peer %q: %w", id.Name, perr)
305			}
306			if perr := mpBind.AddConcentratorPeer(id.Name, id.PSK, psched, pprobers, pfactory); perr != nil {
307				_ = tunDev.Close()
308				return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: wire concentrator peer %q: %w", id.Name, perr)
309			}
310		}
311		dev := awgdevice.NewDevice(tunDev, mpBind, engineLogger(clg, cfg.Log.Level))
312	
313		// Bounded initial hostname resolution (Q30): construct the resolver ONCE (only when some peer
314		// carries a hostname spec — Q29 inertness), resolve each hostname spec under a short timeout,
315		// and seed its expansion. The per-peer boot endpoint — the flattened head of the seeded specs —
316		// is what the UAPI render installs on the engine peer; a hostname that does not resolve in the
317		// boot window (or a resolver that fails to construct) leaves its spec EMPTY and the peer boots
318		// WITHOUT a peer endpoint (tolerant boot). The re-resolution loop then completes the wiring on
319		// first success via the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70). Literal specs are seeded verbatim, so

479	// startFailoverAndResolution builds and starts the edge-side hub-failover monitor AND the
480	// re-resolution controller for cfg's concentrator peer, returning their two SEPARATE stoppers
481	// (device.Close invokes both). It returns no-op stoppers when hub failover does not apply: the
482	// concentrator role (it roams edges dynamically and has no endpoint list), a bind without the
483	// probe transport (no probers → no liveness plane), or no peer whose endpoint set warrants a
484	// controller (peerNeedsHubFailover — a single-IP-literal deployment gets none, staying
485	// behaviour-identical to pre-G5). The edge bonds every path to a SINGLE concentrator, so the whole
486	// per-path prober set IS the liveness of the paths to that concentrator (hub loss = every one
487	// DOWN); the controller drives the first qualifying peer.
488	//
489	// The controller's spec expansions come from boot (the bounded initial resolve, Q30): a hostname
490	// that resolved at boot starts with a non-empty expansion (activeSpec set, endpoint already
491	// installed at boot via the UAPI render), while one that did not resolve starts EMPTY (activeSpec
492	// == -1) and the re-resolution loop's first success adopts it through the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL
493	// PATH (ctrl.install, R70). The install collaborator is wired here to the engine peer.
494	func startFailoverAndResolution(cfg *config.Config, mp *bind.Multipath, probers []*telemetry.Prober, dev *awgdevice.Device, boot bootEndpoints, lg log.Logger) (stopFailover, stopResolution func()) {
495		noop := func() {}
496		if cfg.Role != config.RoleEdge || len(probers) == 0 {
497			return noop, noop
498		}
499		for i, peer := range cfg.WireGuard.Peers {
500			if !peerNeedsHubFailover(peer) {
501				continue
502			}
503			health := make([]hubHealth, len(probers))
504			for j, pr := range probers {
505				health[j] = pr
506			}
507			hlg := lg.Component("hubfailover")
508			// The engine-endpoint install used by the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70) is a REQUIRED
509			// constructor collaborator: boot adoption of an endpoint-less peer installs the resolved
510			// endpoint on the engine peer through it, then rehandshakes. Passing it at construction
511			// (rather than patching a field afterwards) makes a lost wiring line a compile error, so it
512			// can never silently degrade to a SetPeerRemote that leaves the engine peer endpoint-less.
513			ctrl := newHubFailoverFromSpecs(

38	//	(a) it parses nDPI's PER-FLOW output and reads the [Confidence: …] field, failing
39	//	    ONLY on a WireGuard/VPN classification whose confidence is PAYLOAD/CONTENT-based
40	//	    (e.g. "DPI") — a "Match by port" guess is EXPECTED and never fails; and
41	//	(b) it captures the wanbond flow on a NON-registered UDP port (dpiListenPort), so
42	//	    nDPI's port guess does not fire at all and any WireGuard/VPN label on that
43	//	    capture is unambiguously a genuine payload-fingerprint leak.
44	//
45	// Symmetry: the positive control (plain WireGuard) is detected by exactly the
46	// payload/content confidence class ("DPI") that the negative leg must be FREE of, so the
47	// two legs are the same predicate — one asserted present, one asserted absent.
48	//
49	// NON-VACUITY / POSITIVE CONTROL. A bare "not detected" result is meaningless unless the
50	// detector demonstrably DETECTS WireGuard BY PAYLOAD when it is present. wanbond ALWAYS
51	// obfuscates via its outer frame codec, so a plain-WG reference cannot come from wanbond
52	// itself. Option (a) of the task — plain amneziawg-go in-process via tun/netstack — is
53	// NOT tractable: netstack pulls gVisor, whose vendored tcpip/stack fails to build under
54	// the go toolchain (bridge_test.go declares `package bridge_test`), and two kernel-TUN
55	// WireGuard endpoints in ONE netns short-circuit (a packet to the peer's on-link /32
56	// never traverses the WG socket). So this uses option (b): a COMMITTED genuine
57	// plain-WireGuard capture (testdata/plain-wireguard.pcap, real kernel-WG handshake +
58	// transport). nDPI classifies it WireGuard with [Confidence: DPI]; the positive control
59	// asserts that PAYLOAD-confidence classification FIRST — if it is absent, the tool or
60	// parser is broken and the negative assertion is vacuous.
61	//
62	// nDPI carries the WG-SPECIFIC positive control. Suricata 8.x's default configuration
63	// ships NO WireGuard app-layer parser or signature (it labels even a plain-WG flow
64	// app_proto="failed"), so it cannot serve as a WG positive control; its value here is
65	// the app-layer/anomaly NEGATIVE check (with a ">=1 flow decoded" sanity so a zero-decode
66	// run cannot pass silently). This is DOCUMENTED (docs/install.md Limitations and the log
67	// lines below); the suricata positive run is informational only.
68	//
69	// PRIVILEGE. The wanbond capture reuses T26's tcpdump plumbing (startPcap; note the
70	// deliberate absence of `-Z root`, which segfaults tcpdump 4.99.x) over the netns/netem
71	// fixture, so this test needs root/CAP_NET_ADMIN and runs only on the e2e host. It also
72	// needs ndpiReader and suricata ON PATH; if either is absent it FAILS LOUD (never
73	// t.Skip — a skipped DPI check is false assurance). The e2e host must have nDPI and
74	// Suricata provisioned (the orchestrator does this).
75	const (
76		// ndpiReaderBin / suricataBin are the external DPI engines, resolved on PATH.
77		ndpiReaderBin = "ndpiReader"
78		suricataBin   = "suricata"
79	
80		// dpiToolTimeout bounds each external-tool invocation so a hung ndpiReader/suricata
81		// fails the test rather than wedging the suite.
82		dpiToolTimeout = 90 * time.Second
83	
84		// dpiLoadSecs is the bulk-transfer duration driving the wanbond capture: long enough
85		// to put DATA/PARITY/PROBE frames + amnezia junk on the wire for the engines to
86		// inspect, short enough to keep the test quick.
87		dpiLoadSecs = 5
88	
89		// dpiListenPort is the wanbond WireGuard listen port used for the P5 capture. It is a
90		// NON-registered high port ON PURPOSE: capturing on WireGuard's IANA-registered 51820
91		// (the shared listenPort) would make nDPI PORT-GUESS "WireGuard"/VPN regardless of
92		// payload, conflating a port guess with a genuine payload leak. On a non-registered
93		// port nDPI's port guess never fires, so any WireGuard/VPN classification of this
94		// capture is unambiguously a payload-fingerprint defect. wanbond's port is
95		// operator-configurable, so this changes nothing about the acceptance semantics.
96		dpiListenPort = 40000
97	
98		// plainWGPcap is the committed positive-control capture (real plain WireGuard).
99		plainWGPcap = "testdata/plain-wireguard.pcap"
100	
101		// suricataConfigEnv optionally overrides the suricata.yaml path; defaultSuricataConfig
102		// is the standard location a provisioned suricata install writes. When neither is
103		// present, suricata falls back to its own built-in config search.
104		suricataConfigEnv     = "WANBOND_SURICATA_CONFIG"
105		defaultSuricataConfig = "/etc/suricata/suricata.yaml"
106	
107		// suricataEveFile is the JSON event log suricata writes into its -l directory.
108		suricataEveFile = "eve.json"
109	)
110	
111	// vpnLabelPatterns are the lowercase substrings that mark a DPI label as a WireGuard or
112	// otherwise-identified VPN classification. A PAYLOAD-confidence hit on the OBFUSCATED
113	// wanbond flow is a requirement-6 defect; the same on the plain-WG positive control is
114	// the expected teeth. A classification of Unknown/QUIC/DNS/etc. matches none of these.
115	var vpnLabelPatterns = []string{"wireguard", "openvpn", "ipsec", "tunnel", "vpn"}
116	
117	// matchVPNLabel reports the first vpnLabelPattern contained (case-insensitively) in s.
118	func matchVPNLabel(s string) (pattern string, ok bool) {
119		l := strings.ToLower(s)
120		for _, p := range vpnLabelPatterns {
121			if strings.Contains(l, p) {
122				return p, true
123			}
124		}
125		return "", false
126	}

128	func TestP5DPI(t *testing.T) {
129		requireDPITool(t, ndpiReaderBin)
130		requireDPITool(t, suricataBin)
131	
132		bin := buildWanbond(t)
133		top := SetupWithPaths(t, []pathSpec{auditPath})
134	
135		// (1) POSITIVE CONTROL FIRST — prove the detector has PAYLOAD teeth before trusting a
136		// "not classified" result. nDPI must classify the known plain-WireGuard capture as
137		// WireGuard/VPN with a PAYLOAD/CONTENT confidence (not a mere port guess); if it does
138		// not, the tool or parser is broken and every negative below is vacuous.
139		plainPath := plainWGPcapPath(t)
140		posFlows := runNdpiFlows(t, plainPath)
141		if f, label, pat, ok := payloadVPNFlow(posFlows); !ok {
142			t.Fatalf("POSITIVE CONTROL FAILED: nDPI did not classify the known plain-WireGuard capture %s as WireGuard/VPN by PAYLOAD (flows: %s). "+
143				"The detector or the per-flow parser is broken — the negative assertions would be vacuous.", plainPath, formatFlows(posFlows))
144		} else {
145			t.Logf("positive control OK: nDPI classified plain WireGuard as %q by payload (matched %q, %s)", label, pat, f.String())
146		}
147	
148		// (2) Capture the OBFUSCATED wanbond flow (amnezia junk + FEC parity active) on a
149		// NON-registered port and run both engines over it. Neither may classify it
150		// WireGuard/VPN by payload.
151		wbPcap := captureWanbondFlow(t, top, bin)
152	
153		negFlows := runNdpiFlows(t, wbPcap)
154		if f, label, pat, ok := payloadVPNFlow(negFlows); ok {
155			t.Fatalf("REQUIREMENT-6 DEFECT: nDPI classified the OBFUSCATED wanbond flow as a WireGuard/VPN protocol BY PAYLOAD: label %q (matched %q), %s. "+
156				"The flow was captured on non-registered port %d, so this is NOT a port guess — the amnezia+FEC obfuscation is leaking a DPI-identifiable payload fingerprint. "+
157				"Fix the codec; do NOT weaken this matcher. All nDPI flows: %s",
158				label, pat, f.String(), dpiListenPort, formatFlows(negFlows))
159		}
160		t.Logf("nDPI negative OK: obfuscated wanbond flow on port %d — no PAYLOAD WireGuard/VPN classification (flows: %s)", dpiListenPort, formatFlows(negFlows))
161	
162		appProtos, signatures, flows := runSuricata(t, wbPcap)
163		if flows == 0 {
164			t.Fatalf("suricata decoded ZERO flows from the wanbond capture %s — a zero-decode run cannot certify 'no WireGuard/VPN' (check the capture / suricata config), not a pass.", wbPcap)
165		}
166		for _, ap := range appProtos {
167			if pat, ok := matchVPNLabel(ap); ok {
168				t.Fatalf("REQUIREMENT-6 DEFECT: suricata labelled the obfuscated wanbond flow app_proto=%q (matched %q) — a DPI-identifiable VPN fingerprint leaked.", ap, pat)
169			}
170		}
171		for _, sig := range signatures {
172			if pat, ok := matchVPNLabel(sig); ok {
173				t.Fatalf("REQUIREMENT-6 DEFECT: suricata raised a WireGuard/VPN alert on the obfuscated wanbond flow: %q (matched %q).", sig, pat)
174			}
175		}
176		t.Logf("suricata negative OK: %d flow(s) decoded, app_protos=%v, %d alerts — none WireGuard/VPN", flows, appProtos, len(signatures))
177	
178		// (3) Suricata positive is INFORMATIONAL (see the file doc): its default config has
179		// no WG parser, so nDPI carries the WG-specific positive control. Record whichever
180		// outcome this suricata build gives so a provisioned WG ruleset shows teeth.
181		posAppProtos, posSignatures, _ := runSuricata(t, plainPath)
182		if labelsHitVPN(posAppProtos) || labelsHitVPN(posSignatures) {
183			t.Logf("suricata positive (bonus teeth): classified the known plain-WireGuard capture as WireGuard/VPN (app_protos=%v, alerts=%v)", posAppProtos, posSignatures)
184		} else {
185			t.Logf("suricata has NO WireGuard app-layer parser/signature in this build (plain-WG app_protos=%v) — this is expected for the stock suricata config; "+
186				"nDPI carries the WG-specific positive control, suricata provides the app-layer/anomaly negative check (documented in docs/install.md Limitations).", posAppProtos)
187		}
188	}

191	// skips when its engine is missing is false assurance, so this never t.Skip()s.
192	func requireDPITool(t *testing.T, name string) {

195			t.Fatalf("%s not found on PATH: the P5 DPI non-classification check requires it. "+
196				"Provision nDPI/Suricata on this host (the check is never skipped — a skipped DPI check is false assurance). lookup error: %v", name, err)
197		}
198	}

201	// and fails if it is missing.

204		abs, err := filepath.Abs(plainWGPcap)

214	// captureWanbondFlow brings up one fresh amnezia+FEC wanbond tunnel over auditPath on the
215	// NON-registered dpiListenPort, captures the outer UDP wire on the edge veth with tcpdump
216	// (T26's startPcap — no `-Z root`) while a short bulk transfer drives the full
217	// DATA/PARITY/PROBE/junk mix, and returns the path to the completed pcap savefile.
218	func captureWanbondFlow(t *testing.T, top *Topology, bin string) string {
219		t.Helper()
220		pcapFile := filepath.Join(t.TempDir(), "wanbond-obfuscated.pcap")
221		cap := top.startPcap(t, auditPath.edgeVeth, dpiListenPort, pcapFile)
222	
223		edge, conc := setupWanbondDPITunnel(t, top, bin, dpiListenPort)
224		if !top.pingUntil(concInner, 15*time.Second) {
225			cap.stop(t)
226			t.Fatalf("wanbond tunnel never came up\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s", edge.log(), conc.log())
227		}
228		if mbps := top.iperf3Mbps(t, concInner, dpiLoadSecs); mbps <= 0 {
229			cap.stop(t)
230			t.Fatalf("wanbond capture: non-positive throughput %.2f Mbit/s", mbps)
231		}
232		cap.stop(t)
233	
234		if fi, err := os.Stat(pcapFile); err != nil || fi.Size() == 0 {
235			t.Fatalf("wanbond capture %s is missing or empty (err=%v)\n--- tcpdump ---\n%s", pcapFile, err, cap.log())
236		}
237		return pcapFile
238	}

240	// setupWanbondDPITunnel brings the edge+concentrator tunnel up over auditPath with BOTH
241	// the amnezia obfuscation profile (junk active) AND the fixed-ratio FEC plane (parity
242	// active) on a caller-chosen WireGuard listen port. It mirrors setupAuditTunnel but
243	// parametrises the port (the P5 capture runs on a non-registered port to defeat nDPI's
244	// port guess — see dpiListenPort). Addressing/bring-up otherwise match the audit tunnel.
245	func setupWanbondDPITunnel(t *testing.T, top *Topology, bin string, port int) (edge, conc *proc) {
246		t.Helper()
247	
248		edgePriv, edgePub := genKey(t)
249		concPriv, concPub := genKey(t)
250		psk := randKey(t)
251	
252		fecBlock := fmt.Sprintf("[fec]\nenabled = true\ndata_shards = %d\nparity_shards = %d\ndeadline = %d\n\n",
253			auditFECData, auditFECParity, auditFECDeadlineNanos)
254	
255		dir := t.TempDir()
256		edgeCfg := writeConfig(t, filepath.Join(dir, "edge.toml"), fmt.Sprintf(`role = "edge"
257	psk = "%s"
258	
259	[[paths]]
260	name = "%s"
261	source_addr = "%s"
262	
263	%s%s[wireguard]
264	private_key = "%s"
265	
266	[[wireguard.peers]]
267	public_key = "%s"
268	endpoint = "%s:%d"
269	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
270	
271	[log]
272	level = "error"
273	`, psk, auditPath.name, auditPath.edgeIP, amneziaProfileA, fecBlock, edgePriv, concPub, auditPath.concIP, port, concInner))
274	
275		concCfg := writeConfig(t, filepath.Join(dir, "conc.toml"), fmt.Sprintf(`role = "concentrator"
276	psk = "%s"
277	
278	[[paths]]
279	name = "%s"
280	source_addr = "%s"
281	
282	%s%s[wireguard]
283	private_key = "%s"
284	listen_port = %d
285	
286	[[wireguard.peers]]
287	public_key = "%s"
288	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
289	
290	[log]
291	level = "error"
292	`, psk, auditPath.name, auditPath.concIP, amneziaProfileA, fecBlock, concPriv, port, edgePub, edgeInner))
293	
294		conc = top.startProc(t, "concentrator", "nsenter", "-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n", bin, "--config", concCfg)
295		edge = top.startProc(t, "edge", bin, "--config", edgeCfg)
296	
297		if !top.waitLink(tunDev, false, 5*time.Second) {
298			t.Fatalf("edge %s never appeared\n%s", tunDev, edge.log())
299		}
300		if !top.waitLink(tunDev, true, 5*time.Second) {
301			t.Fatalf("concentrator %s never appeared\n%s", tunDev, conc.log())
302		}
303		top.run("ip", "addr", "add", edgeInner+"/24", "dev", tunDev)
304		top.run("ip", "link", "set", tunDev, "up")
305		top.nsenter("ip", "addr", "add", concInner+"/24", "dev", tunDev)
306		top.nsenter("ip", "link", "set", tunDev, "up")
307		return edge, conc
308	}

310	// ndpiFlow is one per-flow classification nDPI reported (from `ndpiReader -v 2`): the
311	// application protocol, its traffic category, and — decisively — the CONFIDENCE with
312	// which nDPI assigned it. A "Match by port" confidence is a PORT GUESS (it says nothing
313	// about the payload); a "DPI"/content confidence is a genuine payload/wire-format match.
314	type ndpiFlow struct {
315		proto      string
316		category   string
317		confidence string
318		raw        string // the trimmed source line, for diagnostics
319	}
320	
321	func (f ndpiFlow) String() string {
322		return fmt.Sprintf("proto=%q cat=%q confidence=%q", f.proto, f.category, f.confidence)
323	}
324	
325	// portGuess reports whether nDPI classified this flow purely by its destination port
326	// (Confidence: "Match by port") rather than by inspecting the payload. Such a guess is
327	// EXPECTED for UDP on WireGuard's registered port and must never fail the negative leg.
328	func (f ndpiFlow) portGuess() bool {
329		return strings.Contains(strings.ToLower(f.confidence), "match by port")
330	}
331	
332	// payloadVPN reports whether this flow is classified as a WireGuard/VPN protocol OR
333	// category by a PAYLOAD/CONTENT confidence — i.e. a genuine wire-format fingerprint, not
334	// a port guess. This is the single predicate both legs use: the positive control asserts
335	// it PRESENT (the detector has payload teeth); the negative leg asserts it ABSENT.
336	func (f ndpiFlow) payloadVPN() (label, pattern string, ok bool) {
337		if f.portGuess() {
338			return "", "", false
339		}
340		for _, c := range []string{f.proto, f.category} {
341			if pat, hit := matchVPNLabel(c); hit {
342				return c, pat, true
343			}
344		}
345		return "", "", false
346	}
347	
348	// payloadVPNFlow returns the first flow that is a payload-confidence WireGuard/VPN
349	// classification, if any.
350	func payloadVPNFlow(flows []ndpiFlow) (flow ndpiFlow, label, pattern string, ok bool) {
351		for _, f := range flows {
352			if label, pat, hit := f.payloadVPN(); hit {
353				return f, label, pat, true
354			}
355		}
356		return ndpiFlow{}, "", "", false
357	}
358	
359	func formatFlows(flows []ndpiFlow) string {
360		if len(flows) == 0 {
361			return "(none)"
362		}
363		parts := make([]string, len(flows))
364		for i, f := range flows {
365			parts[i] = f.String()
366		}
367		return strings.Join(parts, " | ")
368	}
369	
370	// nDPI per-flow field extractors. Each per-flow line (from `-v 2`) looks like:
371	//
372	//	1  UDP a:p <-> b:q [proto: 206/WireGuard][Stack: WireGuard]...[Confidence: DPI][FPC: 0/Unknown, Confidence: Unknown][cat: VPN/2]...
373	//
374	// ndpiFlowConfRE deliberately anchors on `[Confidence:` so it matches the PRIMARY
375	// confidence bracket and NOT the secondary `[FPC: …, Confidence: …]` first-packet-
376	// classification bracket (whose text does not start with "Confidence:").
377	var (
378		ndpiFlowProtoRE = regexp.MustCompile(`\[proto:\s*[^/\]]+/([^\]]+)\]`)
379		ndpiFlowCatRE   = regexp.MustCompile(`\[cat:\s*([^/\]]+)`)
380		ndpiFlowConfRE  = regexp.MustCompile(`\[Confidence:\s*([^\]]+)\]`)
381	)
382	
383	// runNdpiFlows runs `ndpiReader -v 2 -i <pcap>` (very verbose = per-flow detail) under a
384	// timeout and parses the per-flow classification lines. It fails if no flow is found (an
385	// empty capture or a changed output format would otherwise make the negative leg vacuous).
386	func runNdpiFlows(t *testing.T, pcap string) []ndpiFlow {
387		t.Helper()
388		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), dpiToolTimeout)
389		defer cancel()
390		out, err := exec.CommandContext(ctx, ndpiReaderBin, "-v", "2", "-i", pcap).CombinedOutput()
391		if errors.Is(ctx.Err(), context.DeadlineExceeded) {
392			t.Fatalf("ndpiReader timed out after %s on %s\n%s", dpiToolTimeout, pcap, out)
393		}
394		if err != nil {
395			t.Fatalf("ndpiReader on %s failed: %v\n%s", pcap, err, out)
396		}
397		flows := parseNdpiFlows(string(out))
398		if len(flows) == 0 {
399			t.Fatalf("ndpiReader produced no per-flow classification for %s — empty capture or output format changed:\n%s", pcap, out)
400		}
401		return flows
402	}
403	
404	// parseNdpiFlows extracts the per-flow classification lines from ndpiReader's very-verbose
405	// output. A per-flow line is identified by carrying both a `[proto: …]` and a
406	// `[Confidence: …]` field; the extractors pull the protocol name, category, and the
407	// primary confidence.
408	func parseNdpiFlows(out string) []ndpiFlow {
409		var flows []ndpiFlow
410		sc := bufio.NewScanner(strings.NewReader(out))
411		sc.Buffer(make([]byte, 0, 64*1024), 4*1024*1024) // per-flow lines can be long (histograms etc.)
412		for sc.Scan() {
413			line := sc.Text()
414			if !strings.Contains(line, "[proto:") || !strings.Contains(line, "[Confidence:") {
415				continue
416			}
417			f := ndpiFlow{raw: strings.TrimSpace(line)}
418			if m := ndpiFlowProtoRE.FindStringSubmatch(line); m != nil {
419				f.proto = strings.TrimSpace(m[1])
420			}
421			if m := ndpiFlowCatRE.FindStringSubmatch(line); m != nil {
422				f.category = strings.TrimSpace(m[1])
423			}
424			if m := ndpiFlowConfRE.FindStringSubmatch(line); m != nil {
425				f.confidence = strings.TrimSpace(m[1])
426			}
427			flows = append(flows, f)
428		}
429		return flows
430	}

432	// suricataConfigArgs returns the `-c <config>` args to use, if a config is locatable:
433	// the WANBOND_SURICATA_CONFIG override first, then the standard install path. When
434	// neither exists it returns nil so suricata uses its own built-in config search.
435	func suricataConfigArgs() []string {
436		if c := os.Getenv(suricataConfigEnv); c != "" {
437			return []string{"-c", c}
438		}
439		if _, err := os.Stat(defaultSuricataConfig); err == nil {
440			return []string{"-c", defaultSuricataConfig}
441		}
442		return nil
443	}

445	// runSuricata runs `suricata -r <pcap> -l <dir> -k none [-c <config>]` under a timeout and
446	// parses the resulting eve.json into the distinct app-layer protocols, alert
447	// signatures/categories, and the number of flow records it emitted. `-k none` disables
448	// checksum validation so offloaded-checksum captures (veth/loopback) are not dropped.
449	func runSuricata(t *testing.T, pcap string) (appProtos, signatures []string, flows int) {
450		t.Helper()
451		dir := t.TempDir()
452		args := []string{"-r", pcap, "-l", dir, "-k", "none"}
453		args = append(args, suricataConfigArgs()...)
454	
455		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), dpiToolTimeout)
456		defer cancel()
457		out, err := exec.CommandContext(ctx, suricataBin, args...).CombinedOutput()
458		if errors.Is(ctx.Err(), context.DeadlineExceeded) {
459			t.Fatalf("suricata timed out after %s on %s\n%s", dpiToolTimeout, pcap, out)
460		}
461		if err != nil {
462			t.Fatalf("suricata on %s failed: %v\n%s", pcap, err, out)
463		}
464	
465		eve := filepath.Join(dir, suricataEveFile)
466		data, rerr := os.ReadFile(eve)
467		if rerr != nil {
468			t.Fatalf("suricata wrote no %s at %s (a misconfigured suricata, not a pass): %v\n--- suricata output ---\n%s", suricataEveFile, eve, rerr, out)
469		}
470		return parseSuricataEve(t, data)
471	}
472	
473	// suricataEvent is the subset of an eve.json record this check inspects: the event type
474	// (to count decoded flows), the app-layer protocol suricata assigned the flow, and, for
475	// alert records, the rule that fired.
476	type suricataEvent struct {
477		EventType string `json:"event_type"`
478		AppProto  string `json:"app_proto"`
479		Alert     struct {
480			Signature string `json:"signature"`
481			Category  string `json:"category"`
482		} `json:"alert"`
483	}
484	
485	// parseSuricataEve parses newline-delimited eve.json into the distinct non-empty app-layer
486	// protocol values, alert signatures/categories, and the count of "flow" event records.
487	// Robust to the mixed event stream (flow/stats/alert): it keys off event fields.
488	func parseSuricataEve(t *testing.T, data []byte) (appProtos, signatures []string, flows int) {
489		t.Helper()
490		seenProto := map[string]struct{}{}
491		seenSig := map[string]struct{}{}
492		sc := bufio.NewScanner(strings.NewReader(string(data)))
493		sc.Buffer(make([]byte, 0, 64*1024), 4*1024*1024) // eve records can exceed the 64KB default
494		for sc.Scan() {
495			line := strings.TrimSpace(sc.Text())
496			if line == "" {
497				continue
498			}
499			var ev suricataEvent
500			if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(line), &ev); err != nil {
501				// A single malformed line is not a test failure by itself, but surfacing it
502				// aids diagnosis if the format shifted.
503				t.Logf("parseSuricataEve: skipping unparseable eve.json line: %v", err)
504				continue
505			}
506			if ev.EventType == "flow" {
507				flows++
508			}
509			if ev.AppProto != "" {
510				if _, ok := seenProto[ev.AppProto]; !ok {
511					seenProto[ev.AppProto] = struct{}{}
512					appProtos = append(appProtos, ev.AppProto)
513				}
514			}
515			for _, s := range []string{ev.Alert.Signature, ev.Alert.Category} {
516				if s == "" {
517					continue
518				}
519				if _, ok := seenSig[s]; !ok {
520					seenSig[s] = struct{}{}
521					signatures = append(signatures, s)
522				}
523			}
524		}
525		if err := sc.Err(); err != nil {
526			t.Fatalf("scan eve.json: %v", err)
527		}
528		return appProtos, signatures, flows
529	}
530	
531	// labelsHitVPN reports whether any string in ss matches a vpnLabelPattern.
532	func labelsHitVPN(ss []string) bool {
533		for _, s := range ss {
534			if _, ok := matchVPNLabel(s); ok {
535				return true
536			}
537		}
538		return false
539	}

1	//go:build e2e
2	
3	package e2e
4	
5	import (
6		"fmt"

16		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/wireaudit"
17	)
18	
19	// Requirement-6 (DPI-resistance) automated wire-format audit.
20	//
21	// TestWireFormatAudit captures the outer tunnel UDP payloads (the wanbond wire
22	// frames) across >= wireaudit.MinSessions FRESH tunnel sessions — each with amnezia
23	// junk AND fixed-ratio FEC parity ACTIVE, so the capture spans the full traffic mix
24	// (DATA / PARITY / PROBE / CONTROL + amnezia junk) — and then asserts the
25	// requirement-6 obfuscation properties PROGRAMMATICALLY via internal/wireaudit:
26	//
27	//  1. no byte offset holds a constant value across sessions/packets (a DPI
28	//     fingerprint), and
29	//  2. the mean per-packet payload entropy of the large frames exceeds
30	//     wireaudit.MeanEntropyThreshold bits/byte.
31	//
32	// The analysis is a pure, non-privileged helper (internal/wireaudit) with its own
33	// unit tests (planted-constant, entropy-teeth, variable-length, pcap-parse); THIS
34	// test is only the privileged capture+drive wrapper. It also re-proves the
35	// constant-byte detector's TEETH on the REAL captured wire: it plants a constant
36	// byte at a known offset into a copy of the captured frames and asserts the audit
37	// then FAILS and pinpoints that offset.
38	//
39	// Fresh sessions: each session runs in its own t.Run subtest, which stands up a NEW
40	// topology and NEW daemon pair (fresh X25519 keys, fresh PSK => fresh HKDF
41	// obfuscation key => fresh per-frame nonces and keystream) and tears it down before
42	// the next — the fixed veth names forbid two live topologies, so sessions are
43	// strictly sequential. A byte constant WITHIN one session but varying ACROSS
44	// sessions is not a fingerprint; the detector requires cross-session constancy.
45	const (
46		// auditFECData / auditFECParity size the FEC group for the audit. Small K with a
47		// short deadline so parity frames actually appear at the fixture's low frame rate
48		// (the audit needs PARITY on the wire, not a specific recovery ratio).
49		auditFECData   = 4
50		auditFECParity = 2
51		// auditFECDeadlineNanos closes FEC groups after 50ms so parity flushes promptly
52		// even when a group never fills to K at the fixture's low packet rate.
53		auditFECDeadlineNanos = 50 * 1000 * 1000
54	
55		// auditLoadSecs is the per-session bulk-transfer duration: long enough to emit
56		// thousands of MTU-sized DATA frames (so the large-frame entropy sample and the
57		// per-offset sample counts clear their floors) yet short enough for five
58		// sessions to run in reasonable wall time.
59		auditLoadSecs = 5
60	
61		// auditCaptureAttachDelay lets tcpdump open its capture socket and savefile
62		// before any tunnel traffic flows, so the handshake + junk are captured too.
63		auditCaptureAttachDelay = 800 * time.Millisecond
64		// auditCaptureFlushDelay lets trailing packets reach the (packet-buffered)
65		// savefile before tcpdump is signalled.
66		auditCaptureFlushDelay = 400 * time.Millisecond
67	
68		// auditPlantOffset is the byte offset at which the real-wire teeth checks plant a
69		// signature. It sits inside the 24-byte nonce region, present in EVERY frame, so
70		// it is maximally sampled and unambiguously flagged.
71		auditPlantOffset = 10
72		auditPlantValue  = 0x5A
73	
74		// auditMinCoverageOffset is the minimum highest-fully-judged offset the
75		// distribution check must reach: the bulk MTU-sized DATA frames span well past
76		// this, so falling short means a traffic-mix regression shrank the audited region.
77		// Tied to wireaudit.MinEntropyFrameLen — the large-frame region must be covered.
78		auditMinCoverageOffset = wireaudit.MinEntropyFrameLen
79	)
80	
81	// auditLowCardValues is the 4-value round-robin the real-wire low-cardinality teeth
82	// check plants: multi-valued (so the single-valued detector ignores it) yet ~2
83	// bits/byte (so the per-offset distribution check must catch it).
84	var auditLowCardValues = []byte{0x11, 0x22, 0x33, 0x44}
85	
86	// auditPath is the single emulated uplink for the wire-format audit: a low fixed
87	// delay, no jitter, no loss — the audit inspects the OBFUSCATION of the wire, not
88	// its loss behaviour, so a clean path maximises the frame rate and sample size. It
89	// reuses DefaultPaths' veth names/IPs; safe because the test owns its topology and
90	// tears it down between sessions.
91	var auditPath = pathSpec{name: "wan", edgeIP: "10.100.1.1", concIP: "10.100.1.2", edgeVeth: "wbAe", concVeth: "wbAc", delayMs: 10}
92

94		bin := buildWanbond(t)
95	
96		var (
97			mu       sync.Mutex
98			sessions [][]wireaudit.Frame
99		)

312	// startPcap launches tcpdump writing a classic-pcap savefile of the UDP traffic on
313	// the given port over veth (in the current/edge network namespace), packet-buffered
314	// so the file is complete on stop, and waits auditCaptureAttachDelay for the capture
315	// to attach before returning. Requires CAP_NET_RAW (the e2e TestMain namespace / sudo
316	// provides it). It uses tcpdump's DEFAULT privilege drop (to the unprivileged "tcpdump"
317	// user), NOT -Z root: on tcpdump 4.99.x, -Z root segfaults on startup (leaving a 0-byte
318	// savefile). tcpdump opens the -w file as root before dropping, so the root-owned temp
319	// dir is writable, and the savefile is world-readable so the test (root) can read it back.
320	func (top *Topology) startPcap(t *testing.T, veth string, port int, file string) *pcapCapture {
321		t.Helper()
322		out := &lockedBuffer{}
323		cmd := exec.Command("tcpdump", "-i", veth, "-n", "-p", "-U", "-w", file, "udp", "port", strconv.Itoa(port))
324		cmd.Stdout, cmd.Stderr = out, out
325		if err := cmd.Start(); err != nil {
326			t.Fatalf("start tcpdump on %s: %v", veth, err)
327		}
328		cap := &pcapCapture{cmd: cmd, output: out}
329		// Terminate the capture at test end too, in case a fatal path skips stop().
330		t.Cleanup(func() { cap.stop(t) })
331		time.Sleep(auditCaptureAttachDelay)
332		return cap
333	}
334	
335	// setupAuditTunnel brings up the edge+concentrator tunnel over auditPath with BOTH
336	// the amnezia obfuscation profile (junk active) AND the fixed-ratio FEC plane
337	// (parity active) enabled on both ends, so the captured wire carries the full
338	// traffic mix. It mirrors setupP3Tunnel's addressing/bring-up with the [amnezia]
339	// block added.
340	func setupAuditTunnel(t *testing.T, top *Topology, bin string) (edge, conc *proc) {
341		t.Helper()
342	
343		edgePriv, edgePub := genKey(t)
344		concPriv, concPub := genKey(t)
345		psk := randKey(t)
346	
347		fecBlock := fmt.Sprintf("[fec]\nenabled = true\ndata_shards = %d\nparity_shards = %d\ndeadline = %d\n\n",
348			auditFECData, auditFECParity, auditFECDeadlineNanos)
349	
350		dir := t.TempDir()
351		edgeCfg := writeConfig(t, filepath.Join(dir, "edge.toml"), fmt.Sprintf(`role = "edge"
352	psk = "%s"
353	
354	[[paths]]
355	name = "%s"
356	source_addr = "%s"
357	
358	%s%s[wireguard]
359	private_key = "%s"
360	
361	[[wireguard.peers]]
362	public_key = "%s"
363	endpoint = "%s:%d"
364	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
365	
366	[log]
367	level = "error"
368	`, psk, auditPath.name, auditPath.edgeIP, amneziaProfileA, fecBlock, edgePriv, concPub, auditPath.concIP, listenPort, concInner))
369	
370		concCfg := writeConfig(t, filepath.Join(dir, "conc.toml"), fmt.Sprintf(`role = "concentrator"
371	psk = "%s"
372	
373	[[paths]]
374	name = "%s"
375	source_addr = "%s"
376	
377	%s%s[wireguard]
378	private_key = "%s"
379	listen_port = %d
380	
381	[[wireguard.peers]]
382	public_key = "%s"
383	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
384	
385	[log]
386	level = "error"
387	`, psk, auditPath.name, auditPath.concIP, amneziaProfileA, fecBlock, concPriv, listenPort, edgePub, edgeInner))
388	
389		conc = top.startProc(t, "concentrator", "nsenter", "-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n", bin, "--config", concCfg)
390		edge = top.startProc(t, "edge", bin, "--config", edgeCfg)
391	
392		if !top.waitLink(tunDev, false, 5*time.Second) {
393			t.Fatalf("edge %s never appeared\n%s", tunDev, edge.log())
394		}
395		if !top.waitLink(tunDev, true, 5*time.Second) {
396			t.Fatalf("concentrator %s never appeared\n%s", tunDev, conc.log())
397		}
398		top.run("ip", "addr", "add", edgeInner+"/24", "dev", tunDev)
399		top.run("ip", "link", "set", tunDev, "up")

1 package wireaudit 2 3 import ( 4 "encoding/binary" 5 "fmt" 6 ) 7 8 // Classic libpcap savefile constants (the format `tcpdump -w` writes). The layout 9 // is trivial: a 24-byte global header, then a sequence of records each with a 10 // 16-byte header (ts_sec, ts_usec, incl_len, orig_len) followed by incl_len bytes 11 // of link-layer packet data. No external dependency (gopacket etc.) is warranted — 12 // parsing this by hand is a few dozen lines and keeps go.mod minimal. 13 const ( 14 pcapGlobalHeaderLen = 24 15 pcapRecordHeaderLen = 16 16 pcapInclLenOff = 8 // offset of incl_len within a record header 17 pcapLinkTypeOff = 20 // offset of the link-type field in the global header 18 19 // linkTypeEthernet (DLT_EN10MB) is the link type tcpdump reports for a veth. 20 linkTypeEthernet = 1 21 22 ethHeaderLen = 14 23 ethTypeOff = 12 24 ethTypeIPv4 = 0x0800 25 26 ipMinHeaderLen = 20 27 ipTotalLenOff = 2 28 ipFlagsFragOff = 6 // 16-bit flags(3) + fragment-offset(13) field 29 ipProtoOff = 9 30 ipProtoUDP = 17 31 32 // ipFlagMoreFragments (MF) and ipFragOffsetMask isolate the fragmentation state 33 // of an IPv4 datagram. A packet with a non-zero fragment offset carries no UDP 34 // header (its first bytes are payload continuation), and a first fragment with MF 35 // set carries only a truncated payload — either would pool a garbage "frame" into 36 // the audit, so both are skipped. 37 ipFlagMoreFragments = 0x2000 38 ipFragOffsetMask = 0x1FFF 39 40 udpHeaderLen = 8 41 udpSrcOff = 0 42 udpDstOff = 2 43 ) 44 45 // Classic-pcap magic numbers, read as a big-endian uint32 over the first four 46 // bytes. The value encodes both the timestamp resolution and the writer's byte 47 // order, so switching on it yields the record byte order. 48 const ( 49 magicMicroBE = 0xa1b2c3d4 // microsecond ts, big-endian writer 50 magicNanoBE = 0xa1b23c4d // nanosecond ts, big-endian writer 51 magicMicroLE = 0xd4c3b2a1 // microsecond ts, little-endian writer 52 magicNanoLE = 0x4d3cb2a1 // nanosecond ts, little-endian writer 53 ) 54 55 // ParsePcap extracts the UDP payloads whose source OR destination port equals 56 // udpPort from a classic libpcap savefile with Ethernet link-layer framing (what 57 // `tcpdump -i -w file udp port

` produces). Each returned Frame is a 58 // fresh copy of one outer UDP payload — a wanbond wire frame. Non-IPv4, non-UDP, 59 // fragmented, and off-port packets are skipped. Ethernet padding on short frames is 60 // stripped by bounding the payload with the IPv4 total-length field, so a padded 61 // 60-byte minimum Ethernet frame does not contribute spurious trailing zero bytes. 62 func ParsePcap(data []byte, udpPort uint16) ([]Frame, error) { 63 if len(data) < pcapGlobalHeaderLen { 64 return nil, fmt.Errorf("wireaudit: pcap too short: %d bytes < %d-byte global header", len(data), pcapGlobalHeaderLen) 65 } 66 magic := binary.BigEndian.Uint32(data[0:4]) 67 var bo binary.ByteOrder 68 switch magic { 69 case magicMicroBE, magicNanoBE: 70 bo = binary.BigEndian 71 case magicMicroLE, magicNanoLE: 72 bo = binary.LittleEndian 73 default: 74 return nil, fmt.Errorf("wireaudit: not a classic pcap savefile (magic 0x%08x)", magic) 75 } 76 linkType := bo.Uint32(data[pcapLinkTypeOff : pcapLinkTypeOff+4]) 77 if linkType != linkTypeEthernet { 78 return nil, fmt.Errorf("wireaudit: unsupported link type %d (want Ethernet=%d)", linkType, linkTypeEthernet) 79 } 80 81 var frames []Frame 82 off := pcapGlobalHeaderLen 83 for off+pcapRecordHeaderLen <= len(data) { 84 inclLen := int(bo.Uint32(data[off+pcapInclLenOff : off+pcapInclLenOff+4])) 85 off += pcapRecordHeaderLen 86 if inclLen < 0 || off+inclLen > len(data) { 87 return nil, fmt.Errorf("wireaudit: truncated pcap record at byte %d (incl_len %d, %d bytes remain)", off, inclLen, len(data)-off) 88 } 89 pkt := data[off : off+inclLen] 90 off += inclLen 91 if payload, ok := udpPayload(pkt, udpPort); ok { 92 frames = append(frames, append([]byte(nil), payload...)) 93 } 94 } 95 return frames, nil 96 } 97 98 // udpPayload returns the UDP payload of an Ethernet/IPv4/UDP packet whose source or 99 // destination port matches port, or ok=false if pkt is not such a packet. The 100 // payload is bounded by the IPv4 total-length field (not the captured length), so 101 // Ethernet frame padding is excluded. 102 func udpPayload(pkt []byte, port uint16) ([]byte, bool) { 103 if len(pkt) < ethHeaderLen { 104 return nil, false 105 } 106 if binary.BigEndian.Uint16(pkt[ethTypeOff:ethTypeOff+2]) != ethTypeIPv4 { 107 return nil, false 108 } 109 ip := pkt[ethHeaderLen:] 110 if len(ip) < ipMinHeaderLen { 111 return nil, false 112 } 113 ihl := int(ip[0]&0x0f) * 4 114 if ihl < ipMinHeaderLen || len(ip) < ihl { 115 return nil, false 116 } 117 if ip[ipProtoOff] != ipProtoUDP { 118 return nil, false 119 } 120 // Skip any fragment: a non-first fragment (non-zero offset) has no UDP header, so 121 // its payload bytes would be misread as ports and a coincidental match would pool 122 // a garbage frame; a first fragment with MF set is truncated. wanbond frames fit 123 // the path MTU, so a fragmented UDP datagram here is never a real wanbond frame. 124 fragField := binary.BigEndian.Uint16(ip[ipFlagsFragOff : ipFlagsFragOff+2]) 125 if fragField&ipFragOffsetMask != 0 || fragField&ipFlagMoreFragments != 0 { 126 return nil, false 127 } 128 totalLen := int(binary.BigEndian.Uint16(ip[ipTotalLenOff : ipTotalLenOff+2])) 129 // Bound the IP datagram by the captured bytes (guards against a truncated 130 // snaplen or a corrupt total-length field). 131 if totalLen > len(ip) { 132 totalLen = len(ip) 133 } 134 if totalLen < ihl+udpHeaderLen { 135 return nil, false 136 } 137 udp := ip[ihl:totalLen] 138 if len(udp) < udpHeaderLen { 139 return nil, false 140 } 141 src := binary.BigEndian.Uint16(udp[udpSrcOff : udpSrcOff+2]) 142 dst := binary.BigEndian.Uint16(udp[udpDstOff : udpDstOff+2]) 143 if src != port && dst != port { 144 return nil, false 145 } 146 return udp[udpHeaderLen:], true 147 } 148

9	// buildEthIPUDP assembles one Ethernet/IPv4/UDP packet carrying payload between the
10	// given ports, then appends trailing padding bytes (as the NIC pads short frames).
11	// The IPv4 total-length field covers only the real datagram, so a correct parser
12	// strips the padding.
13	func buildEthIPUDP(srcPort, dstPort uint16, payload []byte, padding int) []byte {
14		udp := make([]byte, udpHeaderLen+len(payload))
15		binary.BigEndian.PutUint16(udp[0:2], srcPort)
16		binary.BigEndian.PutUint16(udp[2:4], dstPort)
17		binary.BigEndian.PutUint16(udp[4:6], uint16(len(udp)))
18		// udp[6:8] checksum left zero (valid: UDP checksum is optional over IPv4).
19		copy(udp[udpHeaderLen:], payload)
20	
21		ip := make([]byte, ipMinHeaderLen)
22		ip[0] = 0x45 // version 4, IHL 5 (20 bytes)
23		total := ipMinHeaderLen + len(udp)
24		binary.BigEndian.PutUint16(ip[ipTotalLenOff:ipTotalLenOff+2], uint16(total))
25		ip[8] = 64 // TTL
26		ip[ipProtoOff] = ipProtoUDP
27	
28		eth := make([]byte, ethHeaderLen)
29		binary.BigEndian.PutUint16(eth[ethTypeOff:ethTypeOff+2], ethTypeIPv4)
30	
31		pkt := append(eth, ip...)
32		pkt = append(pkt, udp...)
33		if padding > 0 {
34			pkt = append(pkt, bytes.Repeat([]byte{0xFF}, padding)...)
35		}
36		return pkt
37	}

39	// buildEthIPFragment assembles an Ethernet/IPv4 packet with the raw 16-bit
40	// flags/fragment-offset field set to fragField and the given IP payload (NOT a real
41	// UDP datagram — a fragment carries opaque continuation bytes). proto is set to UDP
42	// so only the fragment guard, not the proto check, can exclude it.
43	func buildEthIPFragment(fragField uint16, payload []byte) []byte {
44		ip := make([]byte, ipMinHeaderLen)
45		ip[0] = 0x45
46		binary.BigEndian.PutUint16(ip[ipTotalLenOff:ipTotalLenOff+2], uint16(ipMinHeaderLen+len(payload)))
47		binary.BigEndian.PutUint16(ip[ipFlagsFragOff:ipFlagsFragOff+2], fragField)
48		ip[8] = 64
49		ip[ipProtoOff] = ipProtoUDP
50	
51		eth := make([]byte, ethHeaderLen)
52		binary.BigEndian.PutUint16(eth[ethTypeOff:ethTypeOff+2], ethTypeIPv4)
53	
54		pkt := append(eth, ip...)
55		return append(pkt, payload...)
56	}

58	// buildPcap wraps packets in a classic libpcap savefile with the given byte order
59	// and Ethernet link type.
60	func buildPcap(bo binary.ByteOrder, magic uint32, pkts [][]byte) []byte {
61		var b bytes.Buffer
62		hdr := make([]byte, pcapGlobalHeaderLen)
63		// The magic constant is the value ParsePcap reads big-endian over the first four
64		// on-disk bytes, so lay it down big-endian regardless of the record byte order.
65		// Everything AFTER the magic is written in the file's declared byte order (bo).
66		binary.BigEndian.PutUint32(hdr[0:4], magic)
67		bo.PutUint16(hdr[4:6], 2)        // version major
68		bo.PutUint16(hdr[6:8], 4)        // version minor
69		bo.PutUint32(hdr[16:20], 262144) // snaplen
70		bo.PutUint32(hdr[pcapLinkTypeOff:pcapLinkTypeOff+4], linkTypeEthernet)
71		b.Write(hdr)
72	
73		for _, pkt := range pkts {
74			rec := make([]byte, pcapRecordHeaderLen)
75			bo.PutUint32(rec[0:4], 1_700_000_000) // ts_sec
76			bo.PutUint32(rec[4:8], 0)             // ts_usec
77			bo.PutUint32(rec[pcapInclLenOff:pcapInclLenOff+4], uint32(len(pkt)))
78			bo.PutUint32(rec[12:16], uint32(len(pkt))) // orig_len
79			b.Write(rec)
80			b.Write(pkt)
81		}
82		return b.Bytes()
83	}

283	// pcapCapture is a running tcpdump savefile capture. It manages its own lifetime
284	// (rather than using startProc, whose cleanup fires only at test end) so the caller
285	// can stop tcpdump and read a complete savefile mid-subtest.
286	type pcapCapture struct {
287		cmd    *exec.Cmd
288		output *lockedBuffer
289		once   sync.Once
290	}

294	// stop flushes trailing packets, signals tcpdump, and waits for it to exit so the
295	// savefile is complete and closed before the caller reads it. Idempotent.
296	func (c *pcapCapture) stop(t *testing.T) {
297		t.Helper()
298		c.once.Do(func() {
299			time.Sleep(auditCaptureFlushDelay)
300			_ = c.cmd.Process.Signal(syscall.SIGTERM)
301			done := make(chan struct{})
302			go func() { _ = c.cmd.Wait(); close(done) }()
303			select {
304			case <-done:
305			case <-time.After(3 * time.Second):
306				_ = c.cmd.Process.Kill()
307				<-done
308			}
309		})
310	}
